senses.definition: The activity of putting plants into pots to grow them.
examples: She spent the afternoon potting seedlings in the greenhouse.
Potting young plants in spring helps them grow strong roots.
We learned basic potting techniques in our gardening class.

senses.definition: The process of covering or embedding electronic parts in a protective material.
examples: Potting protects sensitive components from moisture and dust.
The company uses epoxy potting for all high‑voltage modules.
Good potting can extend the life of electronic devices.

senses.definition: The act of sinking a ball into a pocket in cue sports such as snooker or pool.
examples: Her potting has improved a lot this season.
Accurate potting is essential if you want to win at snooker.
He practised long‑range potting for hours.

senses.definition: A person who sells or makes pots, especially in older or dialect use.
examples: The village pottingar supplied earthenware to all the nearby farms.
Stories mentioned a wandering pottingar who fixed broken jars.
A skilled pottingar could shape clay into many useful forms.

senses.definition: Plural of pottingar; people who sell or make pots.
examples: The market square was busy with pottingars and other traders.
In old records, pottingars are listed alongside smiths and carpenters.
Two skilled pottingars set up workshops near the river.

senses.definition: A family name (surname).
examples: Ms Pottinger will give a lecture on environmental law.
They invited Dr Pottinger to join the research project.
Pottinger has published several articles on finance.

senses.definition: The name of an international consulting firm, especially in investment banking and corporate finance.
examples: Pottinger advised on the merger between the two companies.
She completed an internship at Pottinger in Sydney.
The report was prepared by analysts at Pottinger.

senses.definition: Plural of Pottinger, used for people with the family name Pottinger.
examples: The Pottingers moved to the city last year.
Several Pottingers are buried in the old churchyard.
Three Pottingers attended the reunion.

senses.definition: A container, often made of plastic or cardboard, used for selling food like fruit or yogurt, especially in British English.
examples: She bought a pottle of strawberries at the market.
The yogurt comes in a small pottle with a foil lid.
They shared a pottle of fruit salad on the train.

senses.definition: An old unit of liquid measure equal to half a gallon, or a container of that size.
examples: The recipe calls for a pottle of ale in older versions.
He drank a whole pottle of cider according to the tale.
Merchants once sold wine by the pottle in taverns.

senses.definition: Packed or sold in pottles or small containers.
examples: They sold pottled cream at the country fair.
Fresh, pottled berries were arranged on the stall.
The shop offered pottled desserts for takeaway.

senses.definition: Plural of pottle; small containers used for selling food such as fruit or yogurt.
examples: The stand displayed pottles of blueberries and raspberries.
We bought three pottles of yogurt for the picnic.
Supermarkets often sell ready‑cut fruit in pottles.

senses.definition: A small, slow‑moving African primate with large eyes and a short tail, active mainly at night.
examples: The potto spends most of its time in trees.
Researchers observed a potto feeding on insects after dark.
The zoo recently received a pair of pottos from West Africa.

senses.definition: Plural of potto; small nocturnal African primates with short tails.
examples: Pottos are found in the forests of central Africa.
The reserve protects pottos along with other rare mammals.
Scientists studied how pottos move through the trees at night.

senses.definition: A family name (surname).
examples: Mr Potts will join the panel discussion.
Potts has written several books on education.
The Potts family moved into the house next door.

senses.definition: A historical or dialect word for a potter or maker of pots.
examples: The village pottur fired clay vessels in a simple kiln.
Old records list a pottur living near the riverbank.
A skilled pottur was essential to daily life in the settlement.

senses.definition: Silly or slightly crazy, especially in British informal English.
examples: I thought the idea was completely potty at first.
He must be potty to cycle that far in the snow.
She has some rather potty plans for the weekend.

senses.definition: Relating to or for a young child's use of the toilet.
examples: They bought a new potty chair and some potty books.
Potty training can be stressful for parents and children.
The TV show is full of silly potty jokes for kids.

senses.definition: A small, low bowl or chair with a bowl that a young child uses instead of a toilet.
examples: The toddler sat on the potty for the first time today.
We keep the potty in the bathroom next to the toilet.
Remember to empty and clean the potty after each use.

senses.definition: A person who often uses rude or offensive language.
examples: Her parents told her not to be such a pottymouth.
He used to be a real pottymouth, but he swears less now.
The coach is known as a bit of a pottymouth during games.

senses.definition: Plural of pottymouth; people who often use rude or offensive language.
examples: The comedian joked that his friends were all pottymouths.
Teachers warned the students not to act like pottymouths.
Online forums can sometimes attract pottymouths.

senses.definition: A written abbreviation for President of the United States, often used in news or online.
examples: The POTUS will address the nation this evening.
Journalists waited for comments from POTUS after the meeting.
Social media users reacted quickly to the latest POTUS announcement.

senses.definition: In British history, a person who had the right to vote in some towns because they kept a hearth or cooking pot there.
examples: In the eighteenth century, potwallers formed a large part of the electorate.
The reform act reduced the influence of potwallers in small boroughs.
Historians debate how independent potwallers really were.

senses.definition: Plural of potwaller; people who could vote in certain British towns because they kept a hearth or cooking pot there.
examples: Potwallers were sometimes pressured by landowners during elections.
Some boroughs had only a few dozen potwallers.
The rights of potwallers were limited by later reforms.

senses.definition: The practice or system of giving the vote to potwallers in certain British towns.
examples: Potwalling was criticised for encouraging corruption.
Reformers argued that potwalling distorted representation.
The end of potwalling changed local political life.

senses.definition: Another term for potwaller; a person with a vote in some British towns because they kept a cooking pot and hearth there.
examples: A potwalloper could vote simply by having his own hearth.
The borough was notorious for its potwallopers being bribed.
Reform eventually removed the special status of potwallopers.

senses.definition: Pots and similar ceramic wares considered as a group.
examples: The shop sells simple kitchen potware.
Archaeologists found fragments of Roman potware on the site.
Local potware is displayed in the museum's craft section.

senses.definition: Whisky distilled in a pot still, often used in contrast with whisky from a column still.
examples: Traditional potwhisky is valued for its rich flavour.
The distillery produces both grain whisky and potwhisky.
Connoisseurs often prefer potwhisky for sipping neat.

senses.definition: Work that involves making or decorating pots and similar ceramic items.
examples: Her potwork shows great attention to detail.
He learned basic potwork at an evening class.
The studio displays students' potwork in the entrance hall.

senses.definition: A plant used in the past to flavour or thicken soup or stew.
examples: Villagers gathered potwort to add to their soup.
Old recipes call for potwort and other herbs.
Potwort was once common in kitchen gardens.

senses.definition: A non‑standard or archaic spelling of apothecary, meaning a person who prepared and sold medicines in the past.
examples: The village potycary mixed herbs behind the counter.
An old sign still reads 'John Smith, Potycary'.
She apprenticed with a potycary before becoming a doctor.

senses.definition: In contract bridge, a player who plays poorly or makes obvious mistakes, especially in a casual or humorous way.
examples: He called himself a potzer after misplaying the hand.
The club welcomes beginners and occasional potzers.
Only a real potzer would miss that easy slam.

senses.definition: Plural of potzer; players who make obvious mistakes, especially in bridge, in a light or humorous way.
examples: The tournament is friendly and open to potzers.
We all felt like potzers after that disaster of a game.
Even experts can look like potzers on a bad day.

senses.definition: A French word meaning inch or thumb, sometimes used in English texts referring to French measures.
examples: The old French unit of length, the pouce, is roughly an inch.
He noted the dimensions in pouces and lignes.
Historic plans still show measurements in pouce rather than centimetres.

senses.definition: A rare word possibly referring to a person or tool connected with the unit pouce (inch), used only in very specialised or historical contexts.
examples: The craftsman acted as a poucer, checking widths in old French units.
In some records, a poucer is mentioned among the workshop staff.
Only a specialist would recognise the term poucer today.

senses.definition: Resembling or relating to a pouce (inch), especially in size, in rare or playful use.
examples: The design used small, poucey units of decoration.
He made a poucey sketch using old French measurements.
Only a few poucey marks remained on the ruler.

senses.definition: A small, soft container like a bag, used for carrying small objects.
examples: She keeps her passport in a zip‑up pouch.
Each camera comes with a protective pouch.
He pulled a coin pouch from his pocket.

senses.definition: A pocket of skin on the body of some animals where they carry food or their young.
examples: The baby kangaroo stayed in its mother's pouch.
Hamsters store seeds in their cheek pouches.
The animal used its pouch to protect its young from the cold.

senses.definition: To put something into a pouch or pocket, especially quickly or secretly.
examples: He quickly pouched the coins before anyone saw.
She pouched the letter and left the room.
The guard pouched the keys for safekeeping.

senses.definition: Having a pouch or pouches, or shaped like a pouch.
examples: The pouched rat carries food in its cheeks.
Some pouched mammals keep their young close to the body.
The jacket has a pouched front pocket.

senses.definition: Past tense of pouch; put something into a pouch or pocket.
examples: She pouched the documents and left quickly.
He quietly pouched the winnings.
They pouched the camera in a soft case.

senses.definition: Plural of pouch; small soft containers used for carrying things.
examples: The shop sells pouches for cameras and phones.
She stored spices in little cloth pouches.
Security staff kept the keys in sealed pouches.

senses.definition: Third person singular of pouch; puts something into a pouch or pocket.
examples: He pouches the documents before locking the drawer.
The clerk pouches each passport after checking it.
She pouches the receipts for later.

senses.definition: The amount that fills a pouch.
examples: He poured out a pouchful of coins onto the table.
She brought a pouchful of dried fruit for the journey.
A single pouchful of seeds can feed the birds all day.

senses.definition: Plural of pouchful; several amounts that each fill a pouch.
examples: They carried three pouchfuls of grain back to the village.
He saved several pouchfuls of coins over the years.
Two pouchfuls of rice were enough for the family.

senses.definition: Comparative of pouchy; having more or larger pouches, or looking more like it has pouches.
examples: His eyes looked pouchier after the long flight.
This new design is pouchier and can hold more tools.
The pouchier jacket front suits his style.

senses.definition: Superlative of pouchy; having the most or largest pouches, or looking most like it has pouches.
examples: Those are the pouchiest under‑eye bags I have ever seen.
This is the pouchiest version of the jacket.
The pouchiest design is also the most practical.

senses.definition: The process of putting something into pouches, especially for packaging.
examples: The machine handles the filling and pouching of the product.
Improved pouching reduces packaging waste.
They outsourced the pouching of their coffee beans.

senses.definition: Present participle of pouch; putting something into a pouch or pocket.
examples: She was quietly pouching the coins while we talked.
He stood by the door, pouching his phone and keys.
Workers were pouching samples for the visitors.

senses.definition: Without a pouch or without pouches.
examples: Unlike kangaroos, these animals are pouchless.
A pouchless jacket looks neater but carries less.
The design is simple and pouchless.

senses.definition: Similar in shape to a pouch.
examples: The animal has a pouchlike fold of skin on its belly.
A pouchlike compartment holds tools inside the bag.
They noticed a pouchlike swelling under the leaf.

senses.definition: Looking swollen or baggy, especially under the eyes or in parts of clothing.
examples: He had tired, pouchy eyes after the long flight.
The pouchy pockets make the trousers look casual.
Her face looked a bit pouchy in the morning light.

senses.definition: Having noticeable pouches or sacs, especially in animals.
examples: The pouchy rodents stored food in their cheeks.
This pouchy marsupial keeps its young close.
Pouchy creatures often live in harsh environments.

senses.definition: A rare or non‑standard spelling of pouchy, meaning baggy or swollen, especially under the eyes.
examples: After the sleepless night, his eyes looked poucy.
Her poucy cheeks gave her a tired appearance.
The old dog had a poucy face.

senses.definition: An old or non‑standard spelling of powder.
examples: The recipe calls for a pinch of pouder in the sauce.
On the faded label, the word pouder can still be read.
She dusted her face with a fine cosmetic pouder.

senses.definition: Plural of pouder; old or non‑standard spelling of powders.
examples: Different pouters and pouder were listed in the inventory.
The shop sold healing pouder and scented poudres.
Several cosmetic pouder were displayed on the dressing table.

senses.definition: A French word for powder, sometimes used in English in names of products or recipes.
examples: The chef sprinkled a little sucre en poudre over the cake.
She bought a new face poudre from a Paris brand.
The label read 'poudre fine' in elegant script.

senses.definition: Plural of poudre, used in English contexts that include French names for different powders.
examples: The pharmacist displayed various medicinal poudres.
Her makeup bag was full of scented poudres.
The recipe book mentions several spice poudres.

senses.definition: A small amount or type of powder, especially in older or French‑influenced use.
examples: She dabbed a light poudret on her cheeks.
The perfumer added a floral poudret to the mixture.
A fine poudret settled over the surface.

senses.definition: A powdered mixture made from treated human or animal waste, once used as fertiliser.
examples: Farmers spread poudrette on the fields as manure.
The factory produced poudrette from city sewage.
Concerns about health risks led to a decline in poudrette use.

senses.definition: Very light, powdery snow, often on ski slopes, from the French word for powder.
examples: Skiers enjoyed fresh poudreuse after the night’s snowfall.
The slopes were covered in deep poudreuse.
They dreamed of perfect poudreuse in the mountains.

senses.definition: Plural of poudreuse; areas or stretches of light, powdery snow.
examples: We crossed several untouched poudreuses on the way down.
These slopes are famous for their wide poudreuses.
After the storm, the mountain offered endless poudreuses.
